html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty -- Part One

The Thanksgiving Hymns

HODAYOT -- 1QH

The Hymns of the Teacher of Righteousness and the Community -- DSS Attested

Columns 1 through 4

I Thank You O Creator For You Have Illumined My Face -- You Have Made Me a Banner to the Chosen of Righteousness -- They Have Sharpened Their Tongue Like a Sword -- But You Have Redeemed My Soul From the Pit

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 1 Qumran -- 1QH-a -- Hebrew Primary

One of the Seven Original Scrolls -- 1947 Discovery

Possibly Composed in Part by the Teacher of Righteousness Himself

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

HODAYOT -- THE THANKSGIVING HYMNS -- COLUMNS 1 THROUGH 4

HODAYOT — COLUMN 1 I Thank You O Creator — You Have Illumined My Face With Your Covenant — You Have Made Me a Banner for the Chosen of Righteousness — I Lean on Your Grace — You Have Set Me Apart From All the Sons of Flesh

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have illumined my face by your covenant. I seek you, and sure as the dawn you appear as perfect light to me.

2 You have made me a banner to the chosen of righteousness, an interpreter of knowledge concerning wondrous mysteries.

3 You have given me understanding in your truth and in your wonderful counsel, so that I may exalt your glory and declare your wonders before all your creatures.

4 But I am a creature of clay and kneaded water — a foundation of shame and a spring of impurity, a furnace of iniquity and a structure of sin.

5 A spirit of waywardness and perverted understanding, fearful of your righteous judgments.

6 What am I that you should teach me the counsel of your truth and give me understanding of your wondrous deeds?

7 I lean on your grace and your great compassion — for without you the way cannot be made perfect.

8 And without your will nothing is done. And you have taught me your truth and I have been a servant of your covenant.

9 You have set me apart from all the sons of flesh and you have not made my heart faint.

10 Strengthen the step of your servant in your truth and purify me by your righteousness.

11 And do not let the distresses of my sin overpower me, nor the plots of wickedness overcome me.

12 For you O Creator have I waited, and in the morning I will pray to you and I will understand all your ways forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Hodayot — Thanksgiving Hymns (1QH) was found in Cave 1 at Qumran in 1947 as one of the seven original scrolls. The complete scroll contains twenty-six columns and is designated 1QH-a. Additional fragments were found in Cave 4 (4QH). The Hodayot is one of the longest and most theologically developed documents in the entire Qumran corpus. Column 1 opens with a characteristic Hodayot formula — I thank you O Creator — and establishes the theological paradox that runs through the entire collection: the psalmist is simultaneously a recipient of extraordinary divine revelation and a creature of clay utterly dependent on Creator's grace.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the Tehillim in full — the foundational covenant hymn tradition from which the Hodayot develops. The Hodayot hymns are personal covenant prayers that extend the Tehillim tradition into the specific theological and experiential world of the Qumran covenant community. The paradox of Column 1 — divine illumination received by a creature of clay — reflects the same theological tension that runs through Tehillim 8 — what is man that you are mindful of him — and Tehillim 51 — create in me a clean heart, O Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Tehillim provide the foundational literary and theological model for the Hodayot. The Hodayot develop the Tehillim tradition in three significant ways: they are intensely personal and first-person in a way that exceeds most canonical psalms; they incorporate extensive theological reflection on the community's covenant self-understanding; and they preserve what many scholars believe to be the voice of the Teacher of Righteousness himself — making them potentially the most direct access to the founding figure's inner theological world.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — I am a creature of clay and kneaded water, a foundation of shame and a spring of impurity, a furnace of iniquity and a structure of sin. The Hodayot's anthropological humility is more radical than any other document in the Qumran corpus. Where the Community Rule's Column 11 Hymn of Justification says my justification is with Creator, the Hodayot goes further: the self is described in terms of moral bankruptcy so complete that no human virtue survives. The illumination and revelation received by the psalmist are entirely gifts of grace — there is nothing in the clay creature that merits or invites them.

HODAYOT — COLUMN 2 You Have Made Me a Fountain of Living Water — But the Wicked Have Plotted Against Me — I Lean on Your Truth — The Covenant Community Is a Planting of Cedars — They Shall Stand Forever

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have placed me at a source of flowing streams in a dry land, a spring of water in a parched land, a watered garden.

2 You have planted a planting of cypress and elm and cedar for your glory — trees of life at the secret source, hidden among all the trees of the water.

3 And they shall put forth a shoot of the eternal planting and before they send out their root they shall embrace the stream.

4 And its root shall become exposed in the living water and an eternal spring shall nourish it.

5 But I have been a target of wickedness for all those who seek smooth things — they have roared against me like the roar of great waters.

6 And all their thoughts are the schemes of Belial — they have rolled about in the mire and consulted against me.

7 But you O Creator have helped the soul of the poor and the needy against those stronger than him.

8 You have redeemed my soul from the pit of the lion and from the power of those who seek my life.

9 I know that you are with me in the council of truth and in the holy assembly of your glory.

10 You have illumined my face by your covenant and have purified me from the transgression of men.

11 For all their deeds are before you and none of their devices are hidden from your presence.

12 And I have leaned on your grace O Creator and upon your great compassion, for you are my strength and my fortress and my refuge.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 2 contains one of the most celebrated images in the Hodayot — the planting of trees at the living water, understood as the covenant community established by Creator at the spiritual source. The imagery of the eternal planting — neta olam — is the same phrase used in the Community Rule Column 8 to describe the covenant community itself: it shall be established in truth as an eternal planting, a holy house for Yisra'EL. The psalmist — most likely the Teacher of Righteousness — understands the community he has founded as a divine planting at the source of living water.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Tehillim 1 — blessed is the man who does not walk in the counsel of the wicked — who is like a tree planted by rivers of water. The Hodayot Column 2 develops this Tehillim 1 imagery into an extended meditation on the covenant community as a planting of trees at the living water. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Yesha'yahu 58:11 — you shall be like a watered garden, like a spring of water whose waters do not fail — which stands behind the Hodayot's spring imagery.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yesha'yahu 60:13 — the cypress tree, the pine tree, and the box tree together, to beautify the place of his sanctuary — provides the covenant background for the planting of cypress and elm and cedar in verse 2. The Qumran community applies the Yesha'yahu sanctuary-beautification imagery to the covenant community itself: the community is the living sanctuary whose beauty consists of the spiritual trees planted by Creator at the source of living water.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The personal autobiography embedded in Column 2 — I have been a target of wickedness, they have roared against me, you have redeemed my soul from the pit of the lion — is one of several passages in the Hodayot that most scholars attribute to the Teacher of Righteousness himself. The Teacher appears in the Damascus Document and the Pesher Habakkuk as one who was persecuted by the Wicked Priest. The Hodayot gives us the Teacher's own perspective on that persecution — the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things and those who seek smooth things who roared against him are the same figures the Damascus Document and Pesher Nahum describe from the outside.

HODAYOT — COLUMN 3 I Thank You O Creator For the Wonders You Have Done to Dust and Clay — Through Me You Have Illumined the Faces of the Many — Wondrous Mysteries Have Been Opened to Me — The Foundation of Shame Cannot Conceal the Wonders

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have given me understanding of your truth and have made known to me your wondrous mysteries.

2 And through me you have illumined the faces of the many and shown them your power without limit — the might of your wonders.

3 For you have made known to me your deep counsel and justified me in your wonderful mystery.

4 Who is like you among the heavenly beings O Creator? Your holiness is in the holy abode of the sanctuary.

5 Who is like you among all the holy ones? What is one born of woman in comparison to one who sits on the throne of glory?

6 For you are the Creator of the holy ones and from everlasting ages are the cherubim before the footstool of your glory.

7 And I who am made of clay and kneaded with water — what am I? For I am a foundation of shame.

8 And yet you have opened the fountain of knowledge to me and have shown me your wondrous mystery.

9 And through me you have illumined the faces of the many — for you have given me a spirit of understanding.

10 And you have made me a banner to the elect of righteousness, an interpreter of knowledge for those who walk blamelessly.

11 I shall be a snare to those who sin and a healing to all who repent of transgression and a strength to the simple-hearted.

12 All who have been tried in the furnace of affliction shall praise your name forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 3 contains the Teacher of Righteousness hymns at their most theologically intensive. The repeated self-description as one born of woman or made of clay paired with the description of being given extraordinary revelatory knowledge — through me you have illumined the faces of the many — is the defining paradox of the Teacher hymns: the human vessel is nothing; the revelation given through it is everything. The Maskil figure of the Community Rule — who guides the many in the way of light — is here given his autobiographical voice.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Iyov 25:4 — how can one born of woman be pure — which stands behind the Hodayot's repeated what is one born of woman formula. The Hodayot takes the Iyov question of human unworthiness and transforms it into a covenant paradox: precisely because the human vessel is unworthy, the illumination that flows through it is entirely of divine origin and entirely trustworthy. The Creator of the holy ones has chosen to reveal himself through the weakest of vessels.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yesha'yahu 53:2 — he has no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him — has been seen by some scholars as a background text for the Teacher of Righteousness hymns' repeated emphasis on personal insignificance paired with divine commission. Whether the Teacher understood himself in terms of the Yesha'yahu servant tradition is disputed — but the theological structure is similar: the one who bears the divine revelation is not impressive in human terms.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-11 — I shall be a snare to those who sin and a healing to all who repent of transgression and a strength to the simple-hearted. The Teacher's self-description as simultaneously a snare to the wicked and a healing to those who repent reflects the covenant two-sided function of prophetic proclamation throughout the Hebrew covenant tradition: the word of Creator divides between those who receive it and those who reject it, bringing salvation to the former and judgment to the latter. The Hodayot places this prophetic function in the personal devotional voice of the Teacher himself.

HODAYOT — COLUMN 4 You Have Established Me Against Those Who Oppose Your Covenant — They Have Assaulted My Soul — I Have Been Like a Sailor on a Ship in the Great Sea — But You Have Brought Me to Dry Land — You Have Given Me Understanding

1 I thank you O Creator, for you have sustained me with your might and have poured out your holy spirit upon me so that I cannot stumble.

2 You have established my feet on firm ground and against those who oppose your covenant you have given me strength.

3 For they have assaulted my soul to cause me to exchange your truth for smooth things to your people.

4 But you have made me a reproach and mockery to the treacherous and a foundation of truth and understanding to those who walk blamelessly.

5 I have been like a sailor on the waves of the sea — the billows of death surrounded me and Sheol was on my bier.

6 My spirit was confounded and my soul was overwhelmed with the afflictions of their anger.

7 But you heard the voice of my crying in the distress of my soul and you rescued my soul from the pit.

8 And you have brought me out into a spacious place and saved me and set my feet in a broad place.

9 I lean on your grace and on the abundance of your compassion — for your judgment is not hidden from me.

10 You know the imagination of every spirit and you understand all intentions before you bring them to pass.

11 And without you nothing is done and nothing is known apart from your will.

12 You have taught me your truth O Creator and I know by your spirit what you have revealed to your servants the prophets forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QH Column 4 preserves the Teacher of Righteousness hymns at their most autobiographically intense — the description of assault by those who sought to exchange your truth for smooth things and the image of the sailor tossed on the great sea reflect the historical persecution of the Teacher documented in the Damascus Document and Pesher Habakkuk. The Wicked Priest who pursued the Teacher is here experienced from within — the overwhelming billows of death and Sheol — before Creator rescues the psalmist into a spacious place of covenant safety.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Tehillim 18 — the cords of death encompassed me and the torrents of destruction assailed me — which stands directly behind Column 4's sailor-and-sea imagery. The Hodayot draws on the rescue psalms of the Tehillim tradition and personalises them to the specific covenant experience of persecution and deliverance. The pattern is the same: overwhelming danger, cry to Creator, rescue into covenant safety.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Tehillim 18 and 116 provide the closest parallels to Column 4's personal rescue narrative. The Hodayot's use of the rescue psalm tradition to interpret the Teacher's persecution confirms that the Qumran covenant community understood the Teacher's experience as a covenant typology — what Dawid experienced in his persecution by Sha'ul, the Teacher experienced in his persecution by the Wicked Priest. The covenant pattern of the righteous sufferer vindicated by Creator is the theological framework for both.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — they have assaulted my soul to cause me to exchange your truth for smooth things to your people. The specific accusation against the Teacher is that his opponents wanted him to replace strict covenant teaching with accommodating comfortable interpretation — the same accusation the Pesher Nahum makes against the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things. The Teacher's refusal to give smooth things and his insistence on the demanding truth of the covenant is the primary cause of his persecution. The Hodayot makes this explicit from the Teacher's own perspective.

END OF PART ONE -- COLUMNS 1 THROUGH 4

Volume Forty -- Hodayot -- Part One

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

YOU HAVE PLACED ME AT A SOURCE OF FLOWING STREAMS

IN A DRY LAND A SPRING OF WATER IN A PARCHED LAND

Hodayot Column 2 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 1QH
